title: EdgeRelations

tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG.

  1. If $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ then we call $v_1$ and $v_2$ adjacent in $G$.
  2. Edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_1$.
    Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_2$.
  3. Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ are called out of $v_1$.

lean_blocks.code: def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.sus v1 v2
def edgeInto (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.hus v1 v2
def edgeOutOf (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.tuh v1 v2

addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.
lean_explanation:
The three definitions live in the `CDMG` namespace, so the user-facing names are `CDMG.adjacent`, `CDMG.edgeInto`, and `CDMG.edgeOutOf`; once a `G : CDMG Node` is in scope, Lean 4 dot-notation lets you write `G.adjacent v1 v2` instead of `CDMG.adjacent G v1 v2`.

The right-hand sides are *thin renames* of three primitive operators imported from `CDMGNotation`: `sus` (a symmetric "some edge of any type", spelling the LN macro $\sus$), `hus` (a directed-or-bidirected edge with an *arrowhead at the left argument*, spelling $\hus$), and `tuh` (a directed edge from left to right, spelling $\tuh$). Each of these unfolds to a disjunction of `Finset` memberships in `G.E` / `G.L`; the wrappers here exist purely to give those disjunctions the LN's *names*.

The signatures use Lean 4's implicit binders: `{Node : Type*}` is an implicit universe-polymorphic node type and `[DecidableEq Node]` is an instance argument needed because edge membership is decided over `Finset`s. Both are inherited from a `variable` block, so they do not appear in the `def` headers explicitly.

Crucially, `edgeOutOf` is defined by the *single* operator `G.tuh v1 v2`, not by a disjunction `tuh Ōł© hut`. The LN's two writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the *same* directed edge (`G.hut v1 v2` is by definition `G.tuh v2 v1`), so a disjunction would wrongly include the *reverse* edge $v_2 \to v_1$ as "out of $v_1$".

The choice of `def` (rather than `abbrev`) keeps the names opaque to `simp`, so downstream proofs see `adjacent`, `edgeInto`, `edgeOutOf` as primitives until explicitly unfolded with `simp only [CDMG.adjacent]` etc.
design_choices:
## Design choices for `def_3_3` (EdgeRelations)

**Three two-argument predicates, one per LN item, with the focal vertex first.** The three numbered LN clauses become three separate definitions ŌĆö `adjacent`, `edgeInto`, `edgeOutOf` ŌĆö each taking the graph and two `Node` arguments, with the *first* node argument always being the "focal" vertex (the one the relation is *about*). This unifies the LN's stylistic variation, where item 2 writes the focal vertex on either side and item 3 lists two textual writings of the same edge, into a single uniform Lean shape.

**`def`, not `abbrev`, to keep the named concepts opaque.** All three relations are thin wrappers over the primitive operators from `CDMGNotation` (`sus`, `hus`, `tuh`). Using `abbrev` would cause `simp` to immediately unfold them into their underlying disjunctions and then into `Finset` memberships, defeating the purpose of *naming* the LN's edge-relation concepts. With `def`, downstream proofs in `def_3_4` (walks), `def_3_5` (parent/child/sibling), `def_3_6` (acyclicity), and `claim_3_1` can reason about these as primitives, and a controlled `simp only [CDMG.adjacent]` opens one layer when needed.

**Thin renames of existing operators, not fresh disjunctions.** Each relation is defined as a *single* application of an existing `CDMGNotation` primitive: `adjacent := G.sus v1 v2`, `edgeInto := G.hus v1 v2`, `edgeOutOf := G.tuh v1 v2`. Spelling out the disjuncts would diverge from those primitives and force any future refactor (e.g. adding an edge type) to update multiple sites ŌĆö exactly the failure mode the `CDMGNotation.sus` design guards against.

**Item 3 collapses to a single `tuh`, deliberately *not* `tuh Ōł© hut`.** This is the load-bearing call for the row. The LN lists $v_1 	uh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ as two writings, but the addition `[inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of]` clarifies that these denote the *same edge*. A naive disjunction would be wrong: `G.hut v1 v2` unfolds to `G.tuh v2 v1` ŌĆö the *opposite* directed edge ŌĆö which would falsely classify a `v_2 ŌåÆ v_1` edge as "out of `v_1`". Only `tuh` is used, because bidirected edges have no tail and so cannot be "out of" anything.

**Item 2 picks the focal-on-left `hus` spelling.** Item 2's two sub-clauses ("into $v_1$" vs "into $v_2$") collapse to one predicate parameterised by the focal vertex. The `hus` (focal on the left) spelling was chosen over `suh` because it matches item 2's primary sub-clause orientation, reuses the existing primitive verbatim, and aligns with the walk-into-$v_0$ convention `a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1` in `def_3_4`.

**Edge-as-object abstraction rejected.** A cleaner reformulation taking an edge `Node ├Ś Node` plus a focal vertex was considered and rejected: every downstream consumer pattern-matches on the LN macros (`\hus`, `\suh`, `	uh`, `\hut`) directly, and an edge-object would also force an arbitrary choice between `E`, `L`, or their union.

**Naming and symmetry.** Lowercase camelCase (`adjacent`, `edgeInto`, `edgeOutOf`) mirrors the LN prose and the existing operator names. `adjacent` is symmetric (inherited from `sus`); the two `edge*` relations are *not* symmetric ŌĆö the focal vertex is fixed. Symmetry / equivalence lemmas are deferred to wherever downstream rows actually need them.
